title: Slip-decorated coaster set
subtitle: Four slab coasters with trailing slip patterns in two contrasting colours
excerpt: Four round slab coasters decorated with trailing slip designs: concentric rings, dripped spots, or freestyle lines. Air-dry clay sealed with PVA handles cool dry use. For hot cups, use them as display pieces or drip-tray holders only.
sourceNotes: Slip-trailing technique derived from first principles. Historical slip-trailed earthenware reference: V&A open-access collection (English slipware, 17th-18th century).

content.text: Four round coasters decorated with
slip-trailing
slip

piped or dripped from a small squeeze bottle in patterns that range from disciplined concentric rings to loose, gestural marks. The sealed finished coasters are suitable for cool dry use; they hold cool cups, candles, or small decorative objects. For hot cups, seal with exterior-grade acrylic varnish (two or three coats) and test one coaster with a warm cup before committing the set to daily use.

items.name: White clay for base slip
items.substitution: Tint any base colour with acrylic

items.name: Squeeze bottle or piping bag
items.substitution: A liner brush for spot and line decoration

content.text: Making the coasters
1. Roll and cut four circles
Roll the clay to 8 mm thickness. Cut four 9 cm circles. Let them rest 5 minutes on the board.
2. Apply base slip to the top surface
Brush two coats of white base
slip
onto the top face of each coaster while still soft. Let each coat surface-dry before the next. The base slip gives the trailing slip something to sit on and sets the background colour.
3. Wait for leather-hard
Let the coasters firm to
leather-hard
before trailing. At this stage the base slip is dull-looking and firm; the trailing slip sits on top rather than sinking in and blurring.
4. Trail the decoration
Load the squeeze bottle with a contrasting coloured slip. Try one of these patterns on the four coasters:
Concentric rings: trail a spiral from the outer edge inward, maintaining even pressure.
Spot grid: drip evenly spaced dots in a regular grid pattern.
Freestyle lines: trail loose curves across the surface without a set pattern.
Feather pattern: trail parallel lines across the coaster, then drag a needle through them at right angles.
Drying and finishing

Dry flat on a plaster bat. Once bone-dry, seal the top surface with two coats of PVA and the full coaster (including base) with one coat. Stick a 9 cm felt circle to the base of each to protect surfaces.

items.fix: Wait until the base slip is matte and firm; leather-hard is the correct window
items.cause: Both base slip and coaster body still too wet when trailing
items.symptom: Trailing slip sinks into the base slip and blurs

items.fix: Dry on a plaster bat; weight with a light tile if warping is visible in the early drying stage
items.cause: One face drying faster than the other
